name: Love & Romance
description: The timeless language of the heart, from the greatest poets and writers of love.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: How do I love thee? Let me count the ways.
quotes.notes: The most famous opening line in English love poetry, written for her husband Robert Browning.

quotes.content: I love thee to the depth and breadth and height my soul can reach, when feeling out of sight for the ends of being and ideal grace.
quotes.notes: The opening measure of the poet's catalogue of the ways she loves.

quotes.content: I love thee with the breath, smiles, tears, of all my life; and, if God choose, I shall but love thee better after death.
quotes.notes: The closing lines of Sonnet 43, promising a love that outlasts death itself.

quotes.content: I love you as certain dark things are to be loved, in secret, between the shadow and the soul.
quotes.source: Sonnet XVII, Cien sonetos de amor (1959), trans. Mark Eisner, The Essential Neruda (2004)
quotes.notes: Mark Eisner's widely quoted English rendering of the sonnet Neruda wrote for Matilde Urrutia.

quotes.content: I want to do with you what spring does with the cherry trees.
quotes.source: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair, Poem 14 (1924), trans. W. S. Merwin
quotes.notes: From 'Every Day You Play,' in W. S. Merwin's standard English translation.

quotes.content: I crave your mouth, your voice, your hair. Silent and starving, I prowl through the streets.
quotes.source: Sonnet XI, Cien sonetos de amor (1959), trans. Stephen Tapscott, 100 Love Sonnets (1986)
quotes.notes: The opening of Sonnet XI in Stephen Tapscott's authorized translation.

quotes.content: For one human being to love another: that is perhaps the most difficult of all our tasks, the ultimate, the last test and proof, the work for which all other work is but preparation.
quotes.source: Letters to a Young Poet, Seventh Letter (1929), trans. Stephen Mitchell
quotes.notes: From the letter of 14 May 1904, in Stephen Mitchell's translation.

quotes.content: I hold this to be the highest task of a bond between two people: that each should stand guard over the solitude of the other.
quotes.source: Letters to a Young Poet, Seventh Letter (1929), trans. Stephen Mitchell
quotes.notes: Rilke's ideal of love as two solitudes protecting one another.

quotes.content: To begin by always thinking of love as an action rather than a feeling is one way in which anyone using the word in this manner automatically assumes accountability and responsibility.
quotes.notes: hooks argues that treating love as a verb makes us answerable for how we love.

quotes.content: The moment we choose to love we begin to move against domination, against oppression. The moment we choose to love we begin to move towards freedom.
quotes.notes: hooks frames the choice to love as an act of liberation.

quotes.content: Love is an activity, not a passive affect; it is a standing in, not a falling for.
quotes.notes: Fromm's central claim that mature love is a practiced act of giving, not an accident of attraction.

quotes.content: Immature love says: I love you because I need you. Mature love says: I need you because I love you.
quotes.notes: Fromm's distinction between dependence and freely chosen love.

quotes.content: The supreme happiness of life is the conviction that we are loved; loved for ourselves, say rather, loved in spite of ourselves.
quotes.source: Les Misérables (1862), trans. Charles E. Wilbour
quotes.notes: From 'Fantine,' Book 5; the Wilbour translation of the year of first publication.

quotes.content: Whatever our souls are made of, his and mine are the same.
quotes.notes: Catherine Earnshaw describing the depth of her bond with Heathcliff.

quotes.content: My love for Heathcliff resembles the eternal rocks beneath: a source of little visible delight, but necessary. Nelly, I am Heathcliff!
quotes.notes: Catherine's confession of a love so total it dissolves the boundary of self.

quotes.content: I cannot live without my life! I cannot live without my soul!
quotes.notes: Heathcliff's cry of grief after Catherine's death.

quotes.content: It was inevitable: the scent of bitter almonds always reminded him of the fate of unrequited love.
quotes.source: Love in the Time of Cholera (1988), trans. Edith Grossman
quotes.notes: The novel's famous opening line, in Edith Grossman's English translation.

quotes.content: He allowed himself to be swayed by his conviction that human beings are not born once and for all on the day their mothers give birth to them, but that life obliges them over and over again to give birth to themselves.
quotes.source: Love in the Time of Cholera (1988), trans. Edith Grossman
quotes.notes: The genuine 'give birth to themselves' passage, in Grossman's translation.

quotes.content: He was still too young to know that the heart's memory eliminates the bad and magnifies the good.
quotes.source: Love in the Time of Cholera (1988), trans. Edith Grossman
quotes.notes: On the way memory sweetens the past into longing.

quotes.content: I love her and that's the beginning and end of everything.
quotes.notes: Fitzgerald writing about Zelda in a February 1920 letter, collected in his correspondence.

quotes.content: Love costs all we are and will ever be. Yet it is only love which sets us free.
quotes.source: Touched by an Angel, in I Shall Not Be Moved (1990)
quotes.notes: The closing lines of Angelou's poem on love as both a price and a liberation.

quotes.content: If ever two were one, then surely we. If ever man were loved by wife, then thee.
quotes.source: To My Dear and Loving Husband (1678)
quotes.notes: The opening of one of the earliest great love poems in American literature, published posthumously.

quotes.content: Love is my religion, I could die for that. I could die for you.
quotes.notes: From one of the most celebrated love letters in English, written to Keats's beloved Fanny Brawne.

quotes.content: i carry your heart with me (i carry it in my heart)
quotes.notes: The opening line of Cummings's best-known love poem.

quotes.content: You pierce my soul. I am half agony, half hope.
quotes.notes: From Captain Wentworth's letter to Anne Elliot, one of literature's great declarations of love.

quotes.content: Love is not love which alters when it alteration finds, or bends with the remover to remove.
quotes.notes: Shakespeare's definition of constant love, from the sonnet on the marriage of true minds.

quotes.content: Lovers don't finally meet somewhere. They're in each other all along.
quotes.source: The Essential Rumi (1995), trans. Coleman Barks
quotes.notes: Coleman Barks's widely loved rendering of the 13th-century Persian poet.

quotes.content: For love, all love of other sights controls, and makes one little room an everywhere.
quotes.notes: Donne's image of lovers whose union contains the whole world, published posthumously.
